metadata.title: The Obvious Answer (And Why It’s Incomplete)

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. With AI writing code, the bottleneck migrates from typing to verifying what the AI actually produced. The METR trial showed developers spent 9% of their time visibly cleaning up AI outputs. The real cost is invisible: the mental effort of auditing for subtle logical flaws and race conditions. That hidden verification work is what actually produced the measured 19.4% slowdown.

artifactLines: With AI writing code, the bottleneck migrates from typing to verifying what the AI actually produced.
The METR trial showed developers spent 9% of their time visibly cleaning up AI outputs.
The real cost is invisible: the mental effort of auditing for subtle logical flaws and race conditions.
That hidden verification work is what actually produced the measured 19.4% slowdown.

beats.narration_text: The claim that the bottleneck migrates from typing to verification reframes how I think about my own AI-assisted coding. I want to make the invisible work visible. Help me (1) break my next AI-generated module into the verification levels described, up to integration and race-condition testing, (2) actually time the auditing-for-subtle-flaws work separately from the 9% visible cleanup the METR trial measured, and (3) figure out whether that hidden auditing is where my own version of the 19.4% slowdown is hiding.

beats.narration_text: The invisible work—the mental effort of auditing for subtle logical flaws—is what actually created the 19.4% slowdown. The data from the METR trial showed developers spent 9% of their time “cleaning up” AI outputs, but that’s just the visible work.
